summary: DHH introduces Fizzy, 37signals' new Kanban tool, positioning it as a fresh, simplified take on a concept that has grown cumbersome in existing tools. The product is available both as a paid SaaS and as source-available code under the O'Saasy License, which allows self-hosting but reserves commercial SaaS rights. He argues that software ecosystems need recurring cycles of renewal as even great products accumulate cruft over time. The post emphasizes data portability between SaaS and self-hosted instances, and celebrates the View Source ethos applied to backend code. DHH frames Fizzy as both a useful product and a learning resource for modern Rails development.
key_points: Good software ideas inevitably accumulate cruft and complexity, creating a need for periodic fresh resets of proven concepts
Fizzy uses the O'Saasy License — functionally MIT but with a carve-out reserving SaaS commercialization rights for the creators
Data portability between SaaS and self-hosted instances gives users an escape hatch from vendor lock-in
The full source code and pull request history serves as a learning resource for modern Rails application development
Community contributions flow into both the SaaS product and self-hosted versions equally
money_quotes: Very little software is ever the final word on solving interesting problems.
Even products that start out with great promise and simplicity tend to accumulate cruft and complexity over time. A healthy ecosystem needs a recurring cycle of renewal.
Good ideas can grow cumbersome and unwieldy surprisingly quickly. Fizzy is a fresh reset of an old idea. We need more of that.
That means it's not technically Open Source™, but the source sure is open.
In an age where SaaS companies come and go, pivot one way or the other, I think it's a great reassurance that the source code is freely available.
I'm also just a huge fan of being able to View Source.
key_insight: Software ecosystems need recurring cycles of renewal, and combining source-available code with SaaS gives users both convenience and the reassurance of data portability and self-hosting as an escape hatch.
